text: The surgeon makes an incision in the right flank, which will later serve as the site for the ileostomy.
The surgeon inserts an eleven millimeter trocar into the surgical field, preparing for further laparoscopic access.
A six millimeter flexible trocar is introduced at the six o'clock position on the abdominal surface.
The surgeon places a six millimeter flexible trocar at the twelve o'clock position, complementing the previously inserted trocars for the procedure.
A bicurved grasping forceps is introduced through the flexible trocar positioned at the twelve o'clock location.
A monocurved grasping forceps is introduced through the flexible trocar positioned at the six o'clock location.
The surgeon positions the small bowel above the right liver, preparing the surgical field for the rectal cancer procedure.

The surgeon exposes the origin of the inferior mesenteric vein and uses a monocurve coagulating hook inserted through the flexible trocar at the six o'clock position to free the vein from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon introduces a straight 5mm clipper plier through the flexible port at the six o'clock position and clips the exposed inferior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon introduces monocurve scissors through the flexible trocar at the six o'clock position, following the insertion of previous instruments.
The surgeon dissects the left mesocolon while carefully preserving the integrity of Toldt's fascia, using precise surgical techniques to separate the mesocolon from surrounding structures.
The surgeon works during the entire procedure in ergonomical positions.
The surgeon frees the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin and applies clips to secure it, using precise instrument manipulation.
The surgeon uses monocurve scissors to cut the artery, following its previous clipping.
The surgeon dissects the left mesocolon from the gyrotous fascia, carefully separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon frees the sigmoid and left colon from the lateral parietal wall, separating these structures from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon demonstrates the superior limits of rectal mobilization from the abdominal approach, highlighting the anatomical boundaries on both sides.
The surgeon sections the left mesocolon intercorporeally using a monocurve coagulating hook and monocurve grasping forceps, visibly separating the mesocolon tissue.
The level of colic transection is clearly visible, indicating the completion of this surgical step.
The surgeon continues the procedure using transanal laparoscopy, focusing on the rectal area with laparoscopic instruments visible through the anal canal.
The surgeon introduces the reusable D-port through the anus, preparing for transanal access in the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon applies a silicon cap with three orifices to the surgical site, likely facilitating access or manipulation during the transanal laparoscopy procedure.
A graduated straight grasping forceps is introduced through the three o'clock lumen of the silicon cap, visible within the transanal surgical field.
A monocurve grasping forceps is introduced through the nine o'clock lumen, visible within the transanal laparoscopic field.
A monocurve needle holder with a suture is introduced into the lumen at the three o'clock position during the transanal laparoscopy procedure.
The surgeon applies a purse string suture underluminally, maintaining a distance of two centimeters below the rectal lesion.
The surgeon works ergonomically with no instrument collisions.
The surgeon performs intracorporeal suturing within the rectal lumen, skillfully maneuvering the instruments to avoid collisions between their tips.
The surgeon completes the purse string suture by performing an extracorporeal knot, which is then pushed inside the D-port using surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses monocurve scissors to cut the suture within the lumen, following the placement of the purse string suture below the lesion.
The surgeon introduces the monocurve coagulating hook into the lumen at the three o'clock position, preparing to perform coagulation within the rectal lumen.
The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) by circumferentially delimiting the transection of the rectal mucosa.
The surgeon continues to work ergonomically.
The surgeon performs dissection by transfixing the rectal wall, initially focusing on the posterior aspect of the structure.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection on both sides of the rectal wall, carefully respecting the levator ani muscle plane.
The surgeon joins the two lateral dissections medially, completing the circumferential dissection around the rectal wall.
The surgeon continues the dissection posteriorly and laterally, maintaining the correct cleavage plane to ensure precise separation of tissue layers during the total mesorectal excision.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection of the rectal tissue, completing the circumferential approach while maintaining the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon dissects the anatomical plane between the rectum and prostate in male patients, or between the rectum and vagina in female patients, carefully separating these structures.
During the lateral dissection, the surgeon exposes the inferior rectal artery and sections it between 5 mm clips.
The surgeon sections the peritoneal sheet at the level of the promontorium, visually connecting the current dissection with the previous abdominal laparoscopic dissection.
The surgeon sections the peritoneal sheet on the left lateral side of the pelvis, continuing the dissection within the established cleavage plane.
The surgeon sections the peritoneal sheet on the right lateral side of the pelvis, mirroring the previous dissection on the left side.
The surgeon completes the sectioning of the peritoneal sheet medially, finalizing this step of the procedure.
The final view of the total mesorectal excision (TME) is displayed, showing the completed dissection area with clear anatomical boundaries and structures.
The surgical specimen is pushed into the pelvis and grasped by a grasper inserted transanally, facilitating its removal from the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon removes the D-port and extracts the specimen from the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon sections the left colon at the previously marked segment, completing the division of the colon.
The surgeon inserts a circular anvil of the stapler into the rectal stump and performs a purse string suture to secure it in place.
The surgeon inserts the anvil into the anus and reinserts the D-port, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon inserts a monocurve needle holder with a suture into the lumen at the three o'clock position.
The surgeon performs a purse string suture on the rectal stump, capturing the rectal wall in full thickness.
The surgeon continues to work ergonomically.
The surgeon extracts the suture from the rectal stump, completing the purse string closure around the circular anvil of the stapler.
The surgeon ties an external knot and pushes it inside the D-port, positioning it against the anvil of the stapler.
The surgeon cuts the suture, removes the D-port, and introduces the circular stapler into the anus.
The surgeon performs the anastomosis using the circular stapler, followed by an intraluminal check to ensure proper alignment and integrity of the connection.
The surgeon closes the pelvic peritoneal sheet using a monocurved needle holder, completing the procedure.
The surgeon performs a closure on the right side of the pelvis, extending towards the origin of the main vessels, and completes the procedure with a final intracorporeal knot.
The surgeon performs the closure on the left side, completing the procedure symmetrically to the right side closure previously described.
The surgeon searches for the ileocecal valve and selects a twenty-centimeter ileal loop for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon repositions the small bowel into its natural anatomical position within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon completes the procedure by placing a temporary ileostomy at the level of the single incision site.
